title: Project Overview
description: Start with the README to understand why Stencil exists, which Office formats it supports, and where its current pre-alpha boundaries lie. It introduces the Python API, CLI, optional HTTP service, template syntax, and PDF conversion requirements, giving you the vocabulary for every later step.

title: Public Rendering Contract
description: The package initializer defines the stable import surface, while render.py is the central dispatcher that validates format combinations and selects the correct engine. Read the shared exception hierarchy alongside them because it expresses how unsupported formats and rendering failures cross that public boundary.
languageLesson: Python packages use __init__.py re-exports to present a small public API even when the implementation is split across many internal modules. A shared exception hierarchy lets callers handle broad rendering failures or specific format errors without depending on engine details.

title: Office Format Engines
description: Building on the dispatcher, these three modules implement the actual DOCX, XLSX, and PPTX transformations behind one API. Comparing them reveals the core architectural choice: each Office format gets a specialized engine because its document structure and repetition semantics differ, while callers retain a uniform rendering contract.
languageLesson: DOCX, XLSX, and PPTX are ZIP-based Open Packaging Convention documents containing related XML parts. Format-specific modules isolate those structural differences while allowing shared Jinja-style variables, loops, and conditions at the product level.

title: PDF Conversion Boundary
description: After an Office engine produces a document, pdf.py provides the separate conversion boundary used when the requested output is PDF. Its bounded LibreOffice subprocess design keeps an external system dependency out of the format engines and gives the dispatcher one consistent conversion abstraction.
languageLesson: Python subprocess wrappers should bound execution time, capture failures, and isolate temporary files. Treating an external executable as an adapter keeps process-management concerns separate from document transformation logic.

title: CLI and HTTP Adapters
description: With the local rendering pipeline understood, examine how the Typer CLI and optional FastAPI service adapt it for command-line and HTTP callers. Both remain thin boundaries around the same render contract, preserving the Python API as the source of truth while adding input loading, response handling, health checks, and metrics where appropriate.
languageLesson: Thin adapters are a practical form of dependency inversion: transport-specific code translates inputs and outputs, while reusable domain behavior stays in an ordinary Python function. This makes the same engine available to imports, shell commands, and web requests without duplicating rendering logic.

title: Examples as Contracts
description: The examples guide connects the abstract API to concrete DOCX and PPTX templates and their JSON payloads. Study these artifacts together to see how template placeholders and repeated content map onto application data; they also serve as durable fixtures for the integration behavior covered later.
languageLesson: Example payloads work best as executable documentation when their shape matches realistic template data and the same artifacts are exercised by tests. Keeping templates versioned beside representative JSON makes changes to the rendering contract visible and reviewable.

title: Template Construction Workflow
description: These scripts show how the richer sample templates are generated and how a styled report flows through the Python API into PDF. Following them after the static fixtures demonstrates a reproducible authoring workflow and clarifies which styling belongs in the Office template versus the rendering engine.

title: Behavioral Safety Net
description: The main integration suite verifies the format dispatch, Office renderers, fixture outputs, and PDF path introduced in Steps 2-7. The API tests then confirm that the optional service preserves those semantics while adding HTTP responses, metrics, limits, and error translation, making the test layer the clearest statement of supported behavior.
languageLesson: Integration tests are especially valuable for document engines because correctness spans ZIP containers, XML parts, binary output, and external converters. Shared versioned fixtures make regressions observable at the same boundary users depend on.

title: Package and Typing
description: With runtime behavior established, pyproject.toml explains how office-stencil is packaged for Python 3.12, which dependencies are core or optional, and how the CLI entry point is installed. The version helper and PEP 561 marker complete the distribution contract by exposing installed metadata and declaring inline typing support to downstream users.
languageLesson: A py.typed marker tells type checkers that a distributed Python package ships usable inline annotations. Reading versions from installed package metadata avoids maintaining a second hard-coded version inside the source tree.

title: Release Lifecycle
description: Finish with the automation that turns the tested package into a published release and keeps its dependencies current. The changelog supplies the product history behind that pipeline, linking the DOCX foundation, later XLSX/PPTX engines, PDF conversion, and service adapter into a coherent evolution of the architecture.
languageLesson: GitHub Actions workflows combine event triggers, jobs, and sequential steps to validate and publish artifacts. Dependency automation and a human-readable changelog complement CI by making maintenance inputs and user-visible outcomes explicit.
